D4: add memory-budget alerting (RSS >1GB pages someone) as follow-up, not blocking

missing.what: Cassandra incremental backup gives a timestamp boundary but not snapshot isolation in the SQL sense
constraining the online API is essential for the 50ms p99 latency guarantee
Postgres-with-AGE will not fit at our scale; the sharded Citus-on-AGE variant becomes exotic and defeats the simpler-stack motivation
snapshots must use Cassandra incremental backup with a consistent timestamp boundary; no naive nodetool snapshot on live tables
use JanusGraph on Cassandra for the online side (replaces the earlier Neo4j direction)
no Gremlin endpoint on the analytical side
JanusGraph is the architecture choice but production rollout is conditional on confirmed operational expertise
default-no on Redis cache in v1; reconsider only if production measurements force it (this replaces the earlier defer-until-measurement decision on caching)
scale-fit technology choice vs operational tax on a small team; right-tool-per-workload vs minimizing stack complexity

items.sentence: GRAPH-PROCESSING SYSTEM DESIGN STATE
Throughput: 5k online QPS sustained, 20k burst; 50 concurrent analytical
Snapshots must be transactionally consistent (no torn rows in Parquet)
Writes → JanusGraph/Cassandra → consistent snapshot every 5min → Parquet → DuckDB
JanusGraph composite indexes on enumerated query patterns upfront
No Redis cache in v1; reconsider only if measurements show JanusGraph can't hit 50ms p99
Neo4j for online (scale risk, budget constraints)
Custom graph on RocksDB (engineering cost too high)

items.sentence: GRAPH-PROCESSING SYSTEM DESIGN STATE
Throughput: 5k online QPS sustained, 20k burst; 50 concurrent analytical
Snapshots must be transactionally consistent (no torn rows in Parquet)
Freshness alerts: 6min alert, 10min page
Writes → JanusGraph/Cassandra → consistent snapshot every 5min → Parquet → DuckDB
Online API: constrained REST with predicate indexes on common patterns (closed-world)
Analytical: full SQL via DuckDB, recursive CTEs for graph patterns
Backfill: bulk load path bypassing API, writes directly to Cassandra in batches

Bulk load for backfills: bypass API, write directly to Cassandra via BulkLoaderVertexProgram

Given this state, the single most important gating item we must resolve before any production commitment is: Q2 – confirm whether we have operational expertise to run JanusGraph at production scale or

missing.what: Neo4j has not been benchmarked at 50 billion edges; public references cap at single-digit billions and required professional services
JanusGraph on Cassandra is the most credible scale story among open-source graph stores at our budget
Cassandra incremental backup gives a timestamp boundary but not snapshot isolation in the SQL sense
eventual consistency is acceptable for v1 analytical because there are no audit-style analytical queries in scope
constraining the online API is essential for the 50ms p99 latency guarantee
Postgres-with-AGE will not fit at our scale; the sharded Citus-on-AGE variant becomes exotic and defeats the simpler-stack motivation
an erasure log keyed by user id is itself user data under GDPR but is required to prove erasure happened
GDPR does not require instant erasure; 5-minute analytical erasure window is permissible under 'without undue delay'
online lookups must hit p99 under 50ms and p50 under 10ms
analytical staleness must not exceed 5 minutes (hard data-team requirement)
snapshots must use Cassandra incremental backup with a consistent timestamp boundary; no naive nodetool snapshot on live tables
schema changes are additive-only at the edge-type level; renames or tightenings require migrations
migrations must be idempotent (applying twice is a no-op)
online API exposes only a fixed set of query patterns; new patterns added via migration, not at runtime (closed-world API)
no analytical writes ever; analytical is read-only as a hard rule beyond v1
user-erasure deletes the user's vertices and all edges where the user is an endpoint, in both layers, with analytical propagation within one snapshot window
5-minute analytical erasure window must be documented and visible to analytical users via API and UI
encryption at rest for Cassandra and Parquet; encryption in transit for all client connections; key management via standard org KMS
writes to online restricted to the ingestion service authenticated via mTLS
no cut-over on a Friday, no cut-over before a holiday, no cut-over while any team member is on PTO
physically separate online and analytical workloads
online store is source of truth; analytical is read-only replica refreshed every 5 minutes
use JanusGraph on Cassandra for the online side (replaces the earlier Neo4j direction)
use DuckDB on Parquet for the analytical side
expose different APIs per workload: constrained REST for online, full SQL for analytical
no Gremlin endpoint on the analytical side
shadow-query divergence monitoring at 10-minute interval, alert above 0.1% diff to on-call
JanusGraph is the architecture choice but production rollout is conditional on confirmed operational expertise
drop Apache Spark direction; DuckDB is the analytical engine
append-only erasure log keyed by user id and timestamp, separate from deleted data
SLOs: p99 online latency <50ms, error rate <0.1%, JanusGraph queue depth <100, freshness <6 minutes (alert) / <10 minutes (page)
replay-based migration from a known checkpoint, not dual-write (dual-write rejected due to write-load doubling)
cut-over validation gates: bit-exact result match on holdout, 1.5x throughput test passes, 7-day staging soak; all three are hard gates
default-no on Redis cache in v1; reconsider only if production measurements force it (this replaces the earlier defer-until-measurement decision on caching)
access control: online reads via authenticated REST with row-level access by user identity; analytical reads via SSO restricted to data team users
backfill throughput (hours of bulk load) vs the 5-minute analytical staleness guarantee; during a backfill analytical would be stale by hours or days
scale-fit technology choice vs operational tax on a small team; right-tool-per-workload vs minimizing stack complexity
GDPR right-to-erasure vs right-to-audit obligation; erasure log keyed by user id is itself user data but is required to prove erasure happened
how to handle the analytical staleness window during long backfills (candidate: announce known-staleness window via status banner)
do we have operational expertise to run JanusGraph at production scale, or do we need to hire it (6-month timeline if we hire)
legal interpretation of the erasure log under GDPR; do not ship without an answer
verify Cassandra incremental backup behavior at our intended write rate (open task, blocks production rollout)
